questions.question: What premise of adapter-based VLMs (LLaVA, BLIP-2, Qwen-VL) does Chameleon reject?
options: That transformers need attention
That text and images are two different input paths merging partway in — instead, images become discrete tokens from a SHARED vocabulary, so one sequence and one autoregressive loss cover both
That pretraining needs image data at all
That GPUs can process pixels
questions.explanation: Adapter VLMs route text through embed() and images through encoder→projector. Three consequences: output is text-only, interleaved documents are awkward, and visual/text tokens live in different hidden-space regions.

questions.question: How does a VQ-VAE turn an image into transformer-compatible tokens?
options: It hashes the JPEG bytes

An encoder maps the image to a spatial feature map; each feature is replaced by the index of its nearest entry (L2) in a learned codebook — the integer indices form a discrete alphabet a decoder can render back to pixels
It runs OCR on the image
It samples random integers per pixel
questions.explanation: Training combines reconstruction, commitment, and codebook losses. For Chameleon: a 32x32 feature grid with a K=8192 codebook means one image becomes 1024 discrete tokens.

questions.question: What does Chameleon's combined vocabulary look like?
options: Two separate softmaxes, one per modality
Text BPE tokens (~32000) + image VQ tokens (8192) + modality separators in one ~40192-entry vocabulary — one embedding layer in, one softmax out, whatever the modality
Image tokens replace rare text tokens
A 1M-entry pixel vocabulary
questions.explanation: Every token has a single ID. Separators matter: when the model emits <image>, downstream software knows the next 1024 tokens are VQ indices to send to the decoder for pixel rendering.

questions.question: What capability does early fusion unlock that adapter VLMs structurally cannot offer?
options: Faster text-only inference
Mixed-modality GENERATION — the model can emit image tokens and text tokens in one inference call, choosing the order autonomously (image then text, text then image, or interleaved)
Longer context windows
Better tokenizer compression of text
questions.explanation: Since inference is just next-token prediction in the shared vocabulary, "Draw a cat and describe it" can yield <image> ...1024 tokens... </image> followed by a text description — same decoder, same loss.

questions.question: Which training-stability tricks did Chameleon need to make early fusion work?
options: Lower batch size only
QK-Norm, careful dropout placement, and LayerNorm ordering — token-only multimodal training at scale was unstable without them
Switching to fp64 precision
Freezing the embedding layer
questions.explanation: Mixing modalities in one softmax stresses training dynamics. The lesson's comparison point: Chameleon vs BLIP-2's Q-Former is a trade between unified generation and the cheaper, more stable adapter route.
